did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used Artificial Intelligence methodologies, specifically eleven machine learning models including Support Vector Machine (SVM), k-Nearest Neighbors (KNN), and Subspace KNN, to classify eight types of skin lesions (BCC, SCC, MEL, AK, BKL, DF, NV, VASC). The method involved extracting concatenated signatures from Radial Fourier-Mellin and Hilbert transforms as features for the classifiers.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The article presents original experimental results, specifically the application of eleven machine learning models (including SVM, KNN, and Subspace KNN) to classify eight types of skin lesions using concatenated signatures from Radial Fourier-Mellin and Hilbert transforms. It reports specific performance metrics (accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, precision) on a dataset divided into training and test sets, which constitutes original research rather than a review of prior literature. Note: The paper is marked as 'NOT PEER-REVIEWED' and posted on Preprints.org.

title: Classification of Skin Lesion Images Using Artificial Intelligence Methodologies through Radial Fourier-Mellin, Hilbert Transforms Signatures
brief_summary: This study evaluated eleven artificial intelligence models, including Support Vector Machine and k-Nearest Neighbors variants, to classify eight types of skin lesions: basal cell carcinoma, squamous cell carcinoma, melanoma, actinic keratosis, benign keratosis, dermatofibromas, melanocytic nevi, and vascular lesions. The methodology utilized concatenated signatures derived from Radial Fourier-Mellin and Hilbert transforms applied to RGB and grayscale images as input features. The dataset was randomly split into 80% for training and 20% for testing, with each experiment repeated 30 times to calculate average metrics and standard deviations. Performance was assessed using accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, and precision on the test set. The Subspace KNN model achieved the highest performance, yielding 99.98% accuracy, 99.96% sensitivity, 99.99% specificity, and 99.95% precision. The results indicate that using concatenated signatures from integral transforms is a highly effective approach for automated skin lesion classification.

all_task_types.target: Eight discrete skin lesion classes: basal cell carcinoma (BCC), squamous cell carcinoma (SCC), melanoma (MEL), actinic keratosis (AK), benign keratosis (BKL), dermatofibromas (DF), melanocytic nevi (NV), and vascular lesions (VASC).
all_task_types.details: The paper performs multiclass classification of skin lesion images using eleven machine learning models (including SVM, KNN, and Subspace KNN). The task involves assigning each image to one of eight diagnostic categories based on concatenated signatures from Radial Fourier-Mellin and Hilbert transforms. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, and precision metrics on a test set.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2019 Challenge Training Data
datasets_used.description: The dataset was obtained from the 2019 challenge Training data of the 'International Skin Imaging Collaboration' (ISIC). The original dataset contained 25,331 digital images. The authors performed preprocessing to remove images with noise (hair, measurement artifacts), reducing the count to 9,067 images. Subsequently, a data augmentation procedure (scale and rotation) was applied, resulting in a final dataset of 362,680 images. To homogenize classes, 1,840 images were selected for each of the 8 lesion types, totaling 14,720 images used for the experiments.

use.training: Used for training the eleven machine learning models (SVM, KNN, Ensemble classifiers) after extracting concatenated signatures from Radial Fourier-Mellin and Hilbert transforms. The dataset was randomly split into 80% for training.
use.testing: Used for testing the models. The dataset was randomly split into 20% for testing. Each experiment was repeated 30 times.
use.other: The original ISIC dataset (25,331 images) was filtered to remove noise, resulting in 9,067 images, which were then augmented to 362,680 images. The final experimental set was homogenized to 14,720 images (1,840 per class).

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To divide the dataset into training (80%) and test (20%) sets repeatedly to avoid bias and calculate robust performance metrics.
To report the central tendency and variability of accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, and precision metrics across the 30 repetitions.
Cited as the justification for repeating the experiment 30 times to ensure the reliability of the average metrics.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values or significance tests). It reported mean and standard deviation of performance metrics (accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, precision) over 30 repetitions, citing the Central Limit Theorem as justification for the number of repetitions to avoid bias, but did not perform statistical hypothesis tests to compare models or validate significance.

ISIC 2019 Challenge Training Data (Homog.dataset_description: Dataset derived from ISIC 2019, filtered for noise, augmented, and homogenized to 1,840 images per class (14,720 total). Split into 80% training and 20% testing, repeated 30 times.
ISIC 2019 Challenge Training Data (Homog.notes: Subspace KNN achieved the highest performance. All results are reported as Mean ± 1SD over 30 repetitions.

architecture_used.main_model: Eleven machine learning models: Support Vector Machines (SVM), K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN), and Ensemble Classifiers (Bagged Trees, Subspace KNN). Specifically: Quadratic SVM, Cubic SVM, Fine Gaussian SVM, Medium Gaussian SVM, Fine KNN, Medium Gaussian KNN, Cosine KNN, Cubic KNN, Weighted KNN, Bagged Trees, and Subspace KNN.
architecture_used.input_size: null (Input consists of 444-component one-dimensional signature vectors derived from Radial Fourier-Mellin and Hilbert transforms concatenated with Local Binary Pattern (LBP) features from RGB and grayscale channels)

architecture_details.feature_extraction: Concatenated signatures from Radial Fourier-Mellin and Hilbert transforms, plus Local Binary Pattern (LBP) features extracted via MATLAB's extractLBPFeatures function. Total 444 components.

knn_variants.Cubic KNN: Minkowski distance with exponent p=3, k=10
knn_variants.Weighted KNN: Euclidean distance, k=10, weights based on square inverse of distance

ensemble_variants.Subspace KNN: Random Subspace Method for KNN classification

training_details.dataset_balancing: 1,840 signatures per class (8 classes total = 14,720 signatures)

preprocessing_techniques: Noise removal: Elimination of images containing hair, measurement artifacts, and other noise types to facilitate lesion segmentation.
Data augmentation: Application of scale transformations (100%, 95%, 90%, 85%, 80%) and rotation transformations (45°, 90°, 135°, 180°, 225°, 270°, 315°, 360°) to increase dataset size.
Class homogenization: Random selection of 1,840 images per class to balance the dataset across the eight lesion types.
Color space conversion: Segmentation of RGB images into Red, Green, and Blue channels; conversion of images to grayscale using weighted sum (0.299R+0.587G+0.114B).

Feature extraction (as preprocessing for modeling): Extraction of Radial Fourier-Mellin signatures using Hilbert transforms for rotation invariance; extraction of Local Binary Pattern (LBP) texture features using MATLAB's extractLBPFeatures function; concatenation of signatures into 444-component one-dimensional vectors.

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied to the 9,067 noise-filtered images to increase dataset size to 362,680 images. Scale percentages used: 100%, 95%, 90%, 85%, and 80%. Rotation angles used: 45°, 90°, 135°, 180°, 225°, 270°, 315°, and 360°.
Random selection of 1,840 images for each of the 8 lesion types to balance the dataset, resulting in a final experimental set of 14,720 images.

conclusions: The study concludes that using concatenated signatures derived from Radial Fourier-Mellin and Hilbert transforms is a highly effective approach for the automated classification of skin lesions, offering a viable alternative to direct image-based deep learning methods.

Among the eleven artificial intelligence models tested (including SVM, KNN, and ensemble classifiers), the Subspace KNN model achieved the highest performance, yielding 99.98% accuracy, 99.96% sensitivity, 99.99% specificity, and 99.95% precision on the test set.

The authors suggest that traditional machine learning techniques like SVM and KNN can be effective for skin lesion classification, potentially avoiding the high computational costs and invariance issues associated with Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) when dealing with low-contrast lesions.

The methodology demonstrates the potential for developing non-invasive, automated tools for early detection of skin diseases, including malignant ones like melanoma, by analyzing specific texture and signature features rather than raw pixel data.

The study acknowledges that while results were satisfactory across all models, performance varied, with some models (e.g., Medium Gaussian SVM, Cosine KNN) showing lower sensitivity and precision compared to the top-performing Subspace KNN.
important_remarks: The paper is a preprint (NOT PEER-REVIEWED) posted on Preprints.org, which limits the reliability of the findings compared to peer-reviewed literature.

The dataset was heavily manipulated: the original ISIC 2019 dataset (25,331 images) was filtered to 9,067, then augmented to 362,680, and finally homogenized to exactly 1,840 images per class (14,720 total), creating an artificial balance not present in real-world clinical settings.
The study lacks external validation; it relies solely on the ISIC 2019 training set, raising concerns about generalizability to other populations or imaging devices.
The reported performance metrics are exceptionally high (e.g., Subspace KNN accuracy 99.98%), which may indicate overfitting or data leakage due to the small, homogenized, and augmented dataset size.

No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values) was performed to compare model significance; the authors cited the Central Limit Theorem to justify 30 repetitions but did not test for statistical significance between models.
The methodology excludes images with noise (hair, artifacts), which may not reflect the performance of the model on raw, uncurated clinical data where such noise is common.

The study compares traditional machine learning (SVM, KNN) against a baseline of its own feature extraction rather than against state-of-the-art deep learning models on the same unmodified dataset, limiting the comparative validity of the 'breakthrough' claim.
